message.content:
Differentiator titles and descriptions:
1. Imposition Sensitivity and Social Reservation: Low-group participants habitually restrain expression to avoid imposing, showing social anxiety and context-driven inhibition, unlike the high group's confident, touch-seeking sociability.
Low-group transcripts repeatedly foreground worry about “imposing” on others, being “a hardcore introvert,” and feeling constrained by formal settings (“in a lab with researchers, so I’m holding back”). They second-guess whether feelings are “okay to express,” report being “shy at work,” and describe comfort increases as notable shifts from a baseline of social self-censorship. Even intimate disclosures are framed through careful boundary management and fear of pressuring others (e.g., open-relationship negotiations, proposal planning, updating family without overstepping). In contrast, high-group participants present a default of uninhibited sociability—craving touch, hugging, being “an open book”—and, while acknowledging not wanting to make staff uncomfortable, they do not show the same persistent suppression or imposition anxiety.

2. Emotion-Dominant Processing with “Feelings vs Thoughts” Lane Framing: Low-group participants emphasize emotions overtaking cognition, describing “lanes” of feeling vs thinking and reporting task interference and uncertain euphoria.
Low-group speakers richly metacognize their experience as competing “lanes” for feelings and thoughts, often noting emotions “overwhelming” cognition and “going away and coming back” to tasks, missing items, or diverting gaze. They discuss an “additional lane of feeling” opened by MDMA, wonder whether it persists post-session, and frequently question dose/effect authenticity (“maybe placebo,” “I still don’t know if I’m taking Molly”), alongside reporting muted or absent euphoria. They narrate bodily salience (jaw clenching, warmth) but center emotional inundation as the primary driver of attention. High-group participants, by contrast, more often adopt external control strategies (e.g., breathing to re-focus), describe tasks as “easy” or manageable, and frame the experience through neurochemistry/research lenses rather than prolonged emotional interference with cognition.

Differentiator variable names:
imposition_sensitivity_social_reservation, emotion_dominant_lane_processing
